input.industry: Investment Banking / FinTech M&A and Capital Advisory

company_profile.legal_name: Financial Technology Partners LP (US broker-dealer: FTP Securities LLC; UK entity: FinTech Partners Ltd)
company_profile.geography: Headquartered in San Francisco; offices in New York and London; founder based in Miami
company_profile.employee_count: ~225 as of Dec 2021 (WSJ); current directories list 51-200
company_profile.description: Steve McLaughlin left his post as head of fintech investment banking at Goldman Sachs in 2002 and founded FT Partners from his San Francisco apartment. The firm positions itself as the only investment bank focused exclusively on fintech and has stayed founder-owned for its entire history. McLaughlin ran the firm as its sole managing director for roughly a decade and still touches every mandate. The Wall Street Journal reported the firm generated around $600 million of revenue in 2021 with 225 employees, implying roughly $2.7 million of revenue per head, a figure most bulge-bracket coverage groups cannot match. The firm represents sellers only, negotiates success fees that ratchet up with price, and earned what Dealogic recorded as the largest single advisory fee on record, $250 million, on one 2019 sale. FT Partners states it has not actively solicited clients in over a decade and instead filters hundreds of monthly inbound inquiries down to a limited mandate book, running at roughly 30 transactions a year. The firm also runs a substantial research arm, publishing monthly CEO market updates, quarterly FinTech Insights, an annual Almanac, and, since 2025, a co-authored Global FinTech Report with BCG.

key_facts.label: US regulation
key_facts.value: FTP Securities LLC, FINRA / SIPC member

key_facts.label: UK regulation
key_facts.value: FinTech Partners Ltd, FCA appointed representative (FRN 787501)

key_facts.label: Research franchise
key_facts.value: Monthly, quarterly, and annual fintech publications; BCG co-author

company_profile.evidence_note: Sources used for this sample report: FT Partners' own website, transaction announcements, and published FinTech Insights; Wall Street Journal and Bloomberg reporting on firm financials and fee structures; the firm's regulatory disclosures (FINRA/SIPC membership for FTP Securities LLC, FCA reference numbers for FinTech Partners Ltd); LinkedIn profiles and posts for team composition and recent mandates; LiquidityFinder M&A feed for sector deal context; and KPMG Pulse of Fintech for market data. Figures the firm does not disclose (current revenue, fee schedules, mandate pipeline) are marked as estimates or flagged as gaps rather than filled. On commissioned engagements this evidence base extends to registry pulls (Companies House filing history for the UK entity, FINRA BrokerCheck, SEC EDGAR where relevant) and structured deal data via PitchBook and Grata.

business_model.revenue_model: Success-based advisory fees on sell-side M&A and late-stage private financings, with fee percentages that ratchet upward as the achieved sale price rises. The WSJ has described the fee structures as unusually aggressive even by Wall Street standards. The firm booked a $250 million fee on a single 2019 sale and asserted a claim of roughly 9% of transaction value, over $400 million, on Circle's $4.5 billion SPAC merger, a claim that ended in dispute. McLaughlin also invests personally in advised companies, adding a principal return stream on top of fees.
business_model.value_proposition: Twenty-plus years of single-sector focus gives the firm pattern recognition, buyer relationships, and pricing data that generalist banks rebuild from scratch on each fintech mandate. Founders accept the fee premium because the firm's track record suggests it clears higher prices, and because McLaughlin personally works the deal.
key_activities: Sell-side M&A advisory for fintech companies, typically founder- or sponsor-owned
Private capital raising for late-stage fintech companies (Series C and beyond)
IPO advisory, a niche the firm established with VeriFone in 2005
Proprietary fintech research publishing (monthly, quarterly, annual, plus the BCG co-authored global report)
Hosting VIP conferences and video panels that keep CEOs inside the firm's orbit between transactions
key_resources: McLaughlin's personal brand and Goldman-trained senior banker bench
Two decades of proprietary fintech transaction and valuation data
Inbound deal flow: hundreds of monthly inquiries requiring no outbound sales effort
The research franchise, which functions as permanent top-of-funnel marketing
Regulated entities in both the US (FINRA) and UK (FCA appointed representative)
channels: Inbound referrals from founders, VCs, and prior clients
Research distribution to a CEO and investor subscriber base
Conference sponsorships and hosted events (e.g. FinTech Meetup)
Founder's media presence (WSJ, Bloomberg profiles) and LinkedIn activity
business_model.customer_relationships: High-touch and founder-to-founder. McLaughlin stays personally involved in each deal, which clients cite as a differentiator and which simultaneously constitutes the firm's largest single risk. Relationships often begin years before a mandate through research subscriptions and events.
cost_structure: Senior banker compensation, the dominant cost line for any advisory boutique
Office footprint across San Francisco, New York, and London

chart.title: Selected disclosed FT Partners-advised transaction values
chart.subtitle: From the firm's own announcements; financing rounds and M&A mixed. Illustrates the mandate range, not the full book.

market_overview.narrative: FT Partners sells into the fintech transaction market, so its addressable fee pool moves with fintech M&A and late-stage financing volumes. That market reset hard after 2021 and is now recovering on value while consolidating on volume. KPMG's Pulse of Fintech (data via PitchBook) puts total global fintech investment across VC, PE, and M&A at $238.9 billion in 2021, falling to $95.5 billion by 2024, then recovering to $116 billion in 2025. Within that, fintech M&A specifically rose from $44.6 billion across 829 deals in 2024 to $55.4 billion across 840 deals in 2025. Fewer, larger transactions now carry the market: deal counts have fallen for four consecutive years while capital per deal has risen. This mix shift favors advisors, since larger deals are more likely to carry a mandated sell-side bank, and it particularly favors the $100 million to $5 billion range where FT Partners concentrates.
market_overview.market_size_summary: The relevant market for FT Partners is not fintech investment itself but the advisory fee pool it generates. That pool is not published anywhere. The TAM/SAM/SOM section below constructs an estimate from disclosed deal values and standard fee assumptions, and labels every assumption.
market_overview.regional_notes: The US dominates: $56.6 billion of 2025 fintech investment against $29.2 billion for all of EMEA (KPMG). FT Partners' footprint matches this weighting, with two US offices against one in London. The firm has nonetheless closed landmark deals in emerging corridors, including Fawry's Egyptian IPO and Interswitch's investment from Visa in Africa, and GCC deal flow is a live expansion question given rising regional fintech activity.

chart.title: Global fintech investment (VC + PE + M&A)
chart.subtitle: KPMG Pulse of Fintech, data via PitchBook. The 2025 recovery came on the lowest deal count since 2017: fewer, larger deals.

tam.description: Global advisory fee pool on fintech M&A and late-stage private financings. Constructed as: $55.4B of 2025 fintech M&A value at a blended 1.5-2.5% sell-side fee on the advised portion (assume 60-80% of value carries a mandated advisor), roughly $0.5-1.1B, plus placement fees of 2-4% on the ~$59.5B of $100M+ financing rounds (assume 30-50% advised), roughly $0.4-1.2B, plus IPO advisory, fairness opinions, and buy-side work. Every percentage here is an assumption, not a sourced figure.

sam.description: The segment FT Partners actually competes for: sell-side mandates and financings in the $100M-$5B range, where the firm concentrates and where founder-owned or sponsor-owned sellers pick specialist advisors. Excludes mega-deals above ~$20B, which McLaughlin has stated the firm deliberately avoids, and excludes sub-$100M deals that rarely support its fee structure.

som.description: Anchored to the one hard data point available: WSJ-reported revenue of ~$600M in 2021, a peak year for the fee pool. At ~30 deals per year against a 2025 market of 840 fintech M&A deals plus advised financings, the firm plausibly captures a share of the specialist fee pool in the mid hundreds of millions in a normal year. The firm does not disclose current revenue; this range is inference, not fact.

tam_sam_som.methodology_note: No public source states the fintech advisory fee pool, so this sizing is built bottom-up from KPMG deal values and standard advisory fee benchmarks. The single verified revenue anchor is the WSJ's 2021 figure. Treat the ranges as decision-grade approximations for franchise scale, not as auditable figures. A commissioned engagement would tighten these with PitchBook fee data and league-table cross-checks.

industry_trends.title: Strategics out-acquiring banks
industry_trends.description: The BCG/FT Partners 2026 report records fintech companies out-acquiring banks for the first time on record. Mature fintechs with public currency or profitable balance sheets have become the marginal buyer, widening the buyer universe a sell-side advisor can run.

industry_trends.title: IPO window reopening
industry_trends.description: Q1 2026 produced five US-listed fintech IPOs per FT Partners' own Insights. A functioning IPO market restores dual-track processes, which raise both deal competition and advisory fees, and plays to the firm's IPO advisory heritage dating to VeriFone in 2005.

industry_trends.title: Mix shift to mid-market deal sizes
industry_trends.description: FT Partners' Q1 2026 data shows $1B+ fintech M&A falling to 6 announced deals from a Q3 2025 peak of 22, while $100-500M deals climbed to 34% of volume. The fee pool is migrating toward the firm's core size range even as headline mega-deal fees thin out.

industry_trends.title: Digital assets moving into regulated mainstream
industry_trends.description: US regulatory clarity, including the GENIUS Act on stablecoins in H2 2025, helped digital asset investment nearly double to $19.1B in 2025 (KPMG). FT Partners is visibly positioning here: the Digital Asset $355M financing mandate and its December 2025 tokenization conference both signal coverage intent.

industry_trends.title: AI concentrating capital in fewer winners
industry_trends.description: Financing volume held up in 2025-26 while deal counts fell to multi-year lows, with capital concentrating in category leaders. For advisors this means fewer but larger financing mandates and a growing valuation-narrative role, telling the AI story credibly, in every sale process.

competitors.name: Goldman Sachs / J.P. Morgan / Morgan Stanley fintech groups
competitors.positioning: Bulge-bracket coverage of fintech within broader FIG and TMT franchises; dominate $10B+ transactions and IPO underwriting
strengths: Balance sheet and financing capability
Underwriting licenses for IPOs
Global corporate relationships
Can bundle advisory with lending
weaknesses: Fintech is one coverage vertical among dozens
Conflicts from serving both sides of the market

competitors.name: Evercore / Moelis / Centerview
competitors.positioning: Elite independent advisors with episodic fintech mandates, strongest on large-cap and board-level situations
strengths: Senior-banker model comparable to FT Partners
Board-room credibility on complex situations
No lending conflicts
weaknesses: No dedicated fintech data or research franchise
Compete mainly above FT Partners' sweet spot

competitors.name: Houlihan Lokey
competitors.positioning: Mid-market volume leader across sectors, active in fintech through its FIG and tech groups
strengths: Deal volume machine with deep sponsor coverage
Strong in the $100-500M range now growing fastest
Restructuring capability hedges the cycle
weaknesses: Generalist brand carries less founder pull in fintech
Fee levels anchored well below FT Partners' structures

competitors.name: GP Bullhound
competitors.positioning: European technology advisory and investment firm with meaningful fintech deal flow, especially UK and Nordics
strengths: Strong European founder network
Combined advisory and fund model deepens relationships
Research publications mirror the FT Partners playbook regionally
weaknesses: Smaller US presence limits cross-border buyer reach

competitors.name: Broadhaven Capital Partners
competitors.positioning: Fintech and financial services focused boutique, the closest structural analog to FT Partners' specialist model
strengths: Genuine sector specialization
Credible senior bench from bulge-bracket FIG groups
weaknesses: Fraction of FT Partners' brand gravity and deal count
No comparable research and events machine

competitors.name: KBW (Stifel)
competitors.positioning: Financial services specialist strongest in depository and insurance, overlapping FT Partners on fintech-meets-FIG deals
strengths: Deep bank and insurance buyer relationships
Equity research coverage of public fintechs
weaknesses: Brand anchored to traditional financials rather than growth fintech

competitor_landscape.competitive_intensity_narrative: Competition is intense at the edges of FT Partners' range and thin at its center. Bulge brackets take the mega-deals the firm avoids by choice; volume boutiques take the sub-$100M deals its fee structure prices out. In the $100M-$5B fintech sell-side core, no competitor combines single-sector focus, a twenty-year proprietary dataset, a research franchise, and inbound-only deal flow. The moat is real but personal: it is substantially McLaughlin's, and every credible threat scenario starts with senior talent or the founder himself leaving the field.

customer_segments.name: Venture-backed fintech founders (Series C to exit)
customer_segments.description: The core client. Founder-CEOs of growth-stage fintechs approaching a sale or a large financing round, usually on their first and only exit.
needs: Maximum achievable price from a one-shot event
A buyer list they could not assemble themselves
An advisor whose incentives visibly align with theirs
customer_segments.buying_behavior: Inbound and referral driven, exactly as the firm designed. Founders tolerate ratcheting fees because the fee only ratchets if the price does. Selection often traces back to years of research subscriptions and conference contact.

customer_segments.name: PE and growth-equity sponsors
customer_segments.description: Sponsors exiting fintech portfolio companies or taking minority positions, e.g. the Bain Capital and Great Hill Partners mandate on the Chrome River / Certify merger.
needs: Sector-specific buyer intelligence and valuation benchmarks
Process speed and certainty of close
customer_segments.buying_behavior: Repeat, relationship-based buyers who run formal advisor selection. More fee-sensitive than founders and more willing to use bulge-bracket alternatives, making this the segment where FT Partners' pricing meets the most resistance.

customer_segments.name: Late-stage private fintechs raising capital
customer_segments.description: Companies raising $100M+ rounds who hire a placement advisor, e.g. CAIS ($225M Series C), Digital Asset ($355M, 2025), Gauntlet ($125M).
needs: Access to crossover and strategic investors
Optionality toward a future dual-track exit
customer_segments.buying_behavior: Episodic engagement that functions as a wedge: the financing mandate positions the firm for the eventual sale mandate.

customer_segments.name: Corporates and financial institutions (selective)
customer_segments.description: Occasional buy-side or strategic advisory for acquirers and incumbents, historically the smaller share given the sell-side-only positioning on M&A.
needs: Target identification in unfamiliar fintech subsectors
customer_segments.buying_behavior: Transactional and mandate-specific. The firm's sell-side identity limits how far it can grow this segment without diluting its founder-side brand.

strengths: Only established investment bank with exclusive fintech focus, backed by a twenty-year proprietary transaction dataset
Inbound-only deal flow: hundreds of monthly inquiries with no outbound sales cost
Extreme revenue productivity, roughly $2.7M per employee at the 2021 peak (WSJ figures)
Research and events franchise that markets the firm continuously between deals
Founder ownership permits fee aggression and client selectivity no public or partnership-owned rival can match
weaknesses: Acute key-person concentration: McLaughlin was sole MD for a decade and still touches every deal
Fee structures generate disputes (Circle) and reputational friction the WSJ has covered publicly
Founder's personal investments in advised clients create a standing conflict-of-interest question
Glassdoor CEO approval of 59% suggests internal culture strain relative to elite-boutique norms
No underwriting capability, so IPO mandates cap out at advisory rather than bookrunning economics
opportunities: Reopened IPO window (five US fintech IPOs in Q1 2026) revives dual-track and IPO advisory demand
Digital assets and tokenization deal flow, nearly doubled to $19.1B in 2025, where the firm is already visible
GCC and MEA fintech corridors, extending the Fawry and Interswitch precedents
Sponsor-side products: continuation vehicles and secondaries in fintech portfolios
Monetizing the research franchise directly as a data product
threats: Founder retirement, incapacity, or reputational event, with no disclosed succession plan
Elite boutiques building dedicated fintech teams as the sector's fee pool becomes undeniable
Fee compression if the deal mix stays mid-market, where clients have credible cheaper alternatives
AI eroding the research moat by commoditizing sector intelligence
A renewed fintech downturn: the 2022-24 reset cut the underlying market by 60% from peak

forces.rationale: Every mandate is contested by bulge brackets above, volume boutiques below, and elite independents alongside. FT Partners wins on specialization, not on absence of competition.

Anyone can hang a fintech-advisory shingle; nobody can shortcut twenty years of closed deals, the dataset, and the inbound flywheel. The realistic entrant is not a startup but an existing elite boutique verticalizing into fintech.
The product is senior bankers. Goldman-pedigree fintech MDs are scarce, mobile, and expensive, and the firm's own history shows how hard senior hiring was in its first decade.

One-shot founder-sellers have weak negotiating leverage against a specialist, which is precisely how the fee structure survives. Repeat sponsor clients push back harder, and the Circle dispute shows even founder clients contest fees at the extreme.

Substitutes exist at the margins: in-house corp dev teams, direct investor relationships, lawyer-led small deals, and AI tools eroding the research edge. None currently substitutes for a specialist bank on a competitive $500M sale.

porters_five_forces.overall_attractiveness: Structurally attractive niche for the incumbent that defined it. The economics rest on two pillars, specialization and the founder, and only the first is durable without deliberate succession work.

commercial_risks.title: Key-person dependence on the founder
commercial_risks.description: McLaughlin owns 100% of the firm, was its only managing director for a decade, remains involved in every transaction, and has stated he will neither sell nor take the firm public. No succession plan is publicly disclosed.
commercial_risks.mitigation: The senior MD bench has broadened since 2016 (hires from Index/Morgan Stanley, Goldman, J.P. Morgan). Counterparties should still price the franchise as founder-attached until succession is evidenced.

commercial_risks.title: Fee-structure dispute and reputation risk
commercial_risks.description: The claimed ~9% fee on Circle's $4.5B SPAC merger, over $400M, ended in dispute, and the WSJ has characterized the firm's fees as brazen by Wall Street standards. Public fee friction invites client pushback and litigation.
commercial_risks.mitigation: Track record of cleared prices sustains the pricing so far. Watch for engagement-letter litigation and for sponsor clients migrating to cheaper specialists.

commercial_risks.title: Conflict-of-interest exposure from principal investing
commercial_risks.description: The founder personally invests in companies the firm advises. He frames this as alignment; regulators, opposing counsel, or a burned client could frame it differently.
commercial_risks.mitigation: No public enforcement history identified in this research. A commissioned engagement would run FINRA BrokerCheck and litigation searches to confirm.

commercial_risks.title: Cyclicality of the fintech fee pool
commercial_risks.description: The underlying market fell roughly 60% from its 2021 peak to the 2024 trough (KPMG: $238.9B to $95.5B). Advisory revenue amplifies that swing because deal counts and fee ratchets fall together.
commercial_risks.mitigation: Sell-side-only focus and low fixed costs make the model flexible in downturns; the 2022-24 period evidently did not break it. No countercyclical line (e.g. restructuring) exists.

commercial_risks.title: Senior talent retention
commercial_risks.description: A 59% Glassdoor CEO approval rating sits well below elite-boutique norms, and the firm's economics concentrate in one owner, limiting equity upside for rising MDs.
commercial_risks.mitigation: Above-market cash compensation can hold bankers for a time. LinkedIn monitoring of MD departures is the cheap early-warning indicator.

commercial_risks.title: Vertical erosion by elite boutiques
commercial_risks.description: As the fintech fee pool matures, Evercore-class firms can hire two fintech MDs and credibly contest the upper half of FT Partners' range at lower fees.
commercial_risks.mitigation: The dataset, research franchise, and inbound flow are not replicable by hiring alone. Expect share pressure at the $1B+ end rather than franchise displacement.

commercial_risks.title: Mega-deal drought at the top of the mix
commercial_risks.description: FT Partners' own Q1 2026 data shows $1B+ fintech M&A falling to 6 deals from 22 at the Q3 2025 peak. The largest fee events are exactly the ones thinning out.
commercial_risks.mitigation: The offsetting mid-market shift ($100-500M deals at 34% of volume) suits the firm's historical sweet spot, at lower per-deal fees.

growth_opportunities.title: IPO and dual-track advisory revival
growth_opportunities.description: Five US fintech IPOs printed in Q1 2026. The firm invented its IPO-advisor niche with VeriFone in 2005 and can attach that product to every late-stage client currently weighing exit routes.

growth_opportunities.title: Digital assets and tokenization coverage
growth_opportunities.description: Digital asset investment nearly doubled to $19.1B in 2025 on regulatory clarity. The Digital Asset financing mandate and the firm's December 2025 tokenization conference show the coverage build is already underway.

growth_opportunities.title: GCC and emerging-corridor expansion
growth_opportunities.description: Fawry (Egypt) and Interswitch (Africa) prove the firm can clear landmark deals outside its home markets. Gulf fintech activity and sovereign capital create a corridor where no specialist advisor is yet entrenched.

growth_opportunities.title: Sponsor secondaries and continuation vehicles
growth_opportunities.description: PE holds substantial fintech infrastructure assets acquired in 2020-22 at valuations that block conventional exits. Continuation-vehicle and minority-recap advisory extends existing sponsor relationships into a structurally growing product.

growth_opportunities.title: Research franchise as a data product
growth_opportunities.description: The Insights, Almanac, and BCG report machinery already exists as a cost center that markets the bank. Packaging the underlying dataset as a subscription product would create recurring revenue against a purely transactional base.

growth_opportunities.title: Institutionalizing succession into a sale of the franchise
growth_opportunities.description: The founder says the firm is not for sale. Positions change. A firm doing hundreds of millions in revenue with a broadened MD bench would command a strategic premium from any acquirer wanting instant fintech credibility, if key-person risk were credibly reduced first.

key_questions.question: What happens to the mandate book and the inbound flywheel in the first 12 months without McLaughlin, and what binding arrangements (ownership, non-competes, client coverage) exist today?
What share of engagement letters signed in the last 24 months preserved the ratcheting structure intact, and how did realized fee rates move versus 2019-2021?
Across which advised transactions did the founder or affiliated vehicles hold personal positions, and how was that disclosed to counterparties and consent obtained?
How much of expected next-twelve-month revenue sits in the top three mandates, and what is the historical mandate-to-close conversion rate?
What is MD-level attrition over the trailing three years, and what equity or deferred economics now exist below the founder?
In the last ten contested pitches, which firms were in the final two, and on what basis (fee, relationship, sector data) were losses lost?
Are there open FINRA, SEC, or FCA matters touching FTP Securities LLC or FinTech Partners Ltd, and what does BrokerCheck show beyond the public summary?
What did revenue and headcount actually do in 2022-2024, the period between the WSJ's $600M disclosure and the current recovery, for which no public figure exists?

executive_summary.overview: FT Partners is the defining specialist franchise in fintech investment banking: founder-owned since 2002, sell-side only, inbound-driven, and priced at a premium the rest of Wall Street considers audacious. The verified economics are exceptional, roughly $600M of revenue on 225 people in 2021 per the WSJ, and the moat components (proprietary dataset, research franchise, twenty-year track record) are real and compounding. The franchise nonetheless concentrates to a degree unusual even for boutiques: one owner, one brand, one fee philosophy, and a mandate book the founder still personally touches. The market it serves has passed through a 60% peak-to-trough reset and is recovering with fewer, larger deals and a reopened IPO window, conditions that favor exactly this firm, if it holds its people and its founder.
key_findings: Verified peak economics of ~$600M revenue / 225 staff (2021, WSJ); no public figure exists for 2022-2025, which is itself a diligence flag
The moat is structural (data, research, inbound flow) but the revenue engine is personal, with McLaughlin involved in every deal and owning 100%
Fee aggression is a feature and a liability: a record $250M single fee, and a public dispute over a claimed $400M+ Circle fee
Market conditions have turned favorable: fintech M&A value up 24% in 2025 (KPMG), five US fintech IPOs in Q1 2026, deal mix shifting into the firm's core size range
The credible competitive threat is vertical entry by elite boutiques, not displacement by another specialist
investment_thesis_notes: As a counterparty or competitor benchmark: treat FT Partners' pricing as the ceiling of what specialist positioning can extract, and its inbound model as evidence that research-led marketing beats outbound in advisory
Any transaction involving the firm itself, or any strategy premised on its behavior, should be stress-tested against a no-McLaughlin scenario, because no public evidence retires that risk
executive_summary.recommendation: Engage with the firm as the price-setting incumbent of fintech sell-side advisory. Diligence priorities when transacting with or against it: succession arrangements, realized fee rates post-2021, conflict disclosures around the founder's principal investments, and MD-level retention. The franchise merits its premium today; the qualification is durability, not quality.
